clue: You're starting at 1100 Glendale Blvd — a massive building with a dome that once held 5,300 people. Look at the shape of the roof. What shape is it?
historicalFact: The 0.6-mile walk you're about to take passes a megachurch (1923), a lake that used to be a reservoir (1868), a statue that was planned in bronze but carved in stone (1935), an animated neon sign with 1,300 bulbs, and a sidewalk hall of fame that most people step on without noticing.

clue: Look up at the dome at 1100 Glendale Blvd. Something has been lit on top of it, day and night, since 1923. What is it?
historicalFact: The lighted cross has been a beacon in Echo Park for over a century. Aimee Semple McPherson, who built this temple, insisted it stay lit all night — a signal that she was always watching over the neighborhood. The dome beneath it was one of the largest unsupported concrete spans in North America when built.

clue: Find the cornerstone of the domed church at 1100 Glendale Blvd. The inscription begins 'Dedicated unto the cause of...' What is the very last word of the inscription?
historicalFact: The full cornerstone reads: 'Dedicated unto the cause of inter-denominational and worldwide evangelism.' The woman who carved those words into stone meant them literally — the denomination she founded from this building now operates in 146 countries with over 8 million members.

clue: I was the first woman to preach on the radio. I drew 5,300 every Sunday. In 1926, I disappeared from a beach and a city mourned. Find my temple at 1100 Glendale Blvd — read the cornerstone. How many words are in the full inscription?
historicalFact: Aimee Semple McPherson opened Angelus Temple on New Year's Day, 1923. She pioneered 'illustrated sermons' — full theatrical productions with sets, costumes, and lighting that packed all 5,300 seats. When she vanished from Venice Beach in 1926, two people died searching for her. She reappeared five weeks later walking out of the Mexican desert, claiming kidnapping. Witnesses placed her in a Carmel cottage with her married radio engineer.

clue: The domed church at 1100 Glendale Blvd was built by a Canadian woman in 1923. The bust near the lake was placed by Cuban exiles in 1976. Both are declarations carved into permanent materials by communities of newcomers. Read the longer inscription on the bust — it ends with an imperative verb in Spanish, a command to those without a homeland. What is the last word of that inscription?
historicalFact: 'El que tenga Patria que la honre, el que no, que la conquiste' — 'He who has a homeland, honor it; he who does not, conquer one.' McPherson carved her mission into a cornerstone. The Cuban community carved their rallying cry into a pedestal. Fifty-three years apart, same neighborhood, same impulse: write it in stone so it lasts.

clue: You've arrived at the lake. Look at the water. There's a feature shooting up from the middle of the lake. What is it?
historicalFact: This lake started as a reservoir in 1868, feeding a woolen factory at 6th and Figueroa — miles from here. A parks superintendent converted it to recreation in 1892 after he shouted across the water and heard his own voice come back. He named the park after the sound.

clue: What kind of boats are on the lake?
historicalFact: Pedal boats have circled this lake since 1932. The boathouse was built with Depression-era unemployment bonds — a public works project that gave people jobs building a place for other people to relax. It's still here, still renting boats, 94 years later.

clue: Find the 14-foot statue near the boathouse. Her pedestal has carved panels on each side, each depicting a different LA landmark. How many panels are there?
historicalFact: The four bas-reliefs depict the Hollywood Bowl, LA Harbor, the San Gabriel Mountains, and the LA Central Library — a love letter to the city, carved into concrete by a 30-year-old sculptor from Hawaii in 1935.

clue: Look at the pedestal panels on the statue. Each depicts a different LA landmark. One shows a venue where orchestras perform outdoors. One shows a place where ships dock. One shows a mountain range. One shows a building full of books. Name the building full of books.
historicalFact: The LA Central Library depicted in the bas-relief was designed by Bertram Goodhue and completed in 1926. The sculptor carved it into the pedestal as one of four symbols of Los Angeles. The real building survived two devastating arsons in 1986 — both set by the same man.

clue: The statue was planned in bronze, but Depression-era budget cuts forced the sculptor to use something cheaper. Look at the statue — it's not metal. What material is it actually made of?
historicalFact: Ada Mae Sharpless was born in Hilo, Hawaii in 1904 and trained at the Ecole des Beaux-Arts in Paris. She was 30 when she carved the Queen of the Angels from 'liquid stone' — poured concrete — because the WPA budget for bronze ran out. The statue was vandalized, lost her fingers, spent 13 years in storage, and came back in 1999 sealed with anti-graffiti coating. She's tougher than she looks.

clue: You're almost at the bar. Look down at the sidewalk — really look. Bronze plaques are embedded in the concrete, honoring famous people. Find one. What kind of people do the plaques honor — musicians, actors, or athletes?
historicalFact: You're standing on the Avenue of the Athletes — 32 brass plaques honoring Olympians and sports legends, embedded in 10 blocks of Sunset Blvd sidewalk. A Dodgers photographer named L. Andrew Castle created the project in 1976. He died two years later and the plaque designs were literally lost for years.

clue: At 1714 W Sunset Blvd, a storefront window displays two unlikely figures shaking hands — one from the distant past, one from the distant future. A caveman is greeting someone. Who is the caveman greeting?
historicalFact: The Echo Park Time Travel Mart is 'a convenience store for time travelers' selling Viking Odorant, Robot Milk, and Mammoth Chunks. Behind a secret door in the back: a free tutoring center run by 826LA, a nonprofit co-founded by Dave Eggers. Every purchase funds writing education for kids ages 6-18.

clue: At 1706 W Sunset, look up at the rooftop. A massive animated sign uses bulbs in exactly three colors. Name the three colors.
historicalFact: The Jensen's sign uses 1,300 incandescent bulbs — red, green, and white — with electric motors that switch them on and off to simulate a bowler throwing a strike. It's billed as the last articulated incandescent sign in America. It went dark for 50 years before being restored.

clue: The animated sign at 1706 W Sunset depicts a figure mid-motion. What activity is the figure performing?
historicalFact: Henry Christian Jensen built this three-story recreation center in 1924: bowling and pool at street level, 46 apartments with Murphy beds above. Jensen was a German immigrant who made his fortune manufacturing bricks — the same material the building is made of. He literally built his building with his own product.

clue: The building at 1706 W Sunset was built by a man who manufactured one material — the material that LA needed more of than anything as the city expanded in the early 1900s. He used that same material to build this recreation center. Look at the building. What material is it made of?
historicalFact: Henry Christian Jensen manufactured bricks for LA's rapid expansion — his product literally built the city. Then in 1924, he built himself a three-story recreation center at 1706 W Sunset using his own bricks. Bowling downstairs, 46 apartments upstairs. The building still stands. The rooftop sign is now a protected historic landmark.
